text:
FOUNDATION MEDICINE — FoundationOne CDx
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT

PATIENT INFORMATION
Name: Maria Elena Reyes
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Female
Diagnosis (submitted): Invasive ductal carcinoma, left breast, triple-negative, Grade 3
Ordering Physician: [Breast Medical Oncology]
Institution: [Academic Medical Center, Southwestern United States]

SPECIMEN INFORMATION
Specimen ID: [LAB-XXXXXX]
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) core needle biopsy tissue
Anatomic Site: Left breast, 10 o'clock position
Date of Biopsy: [on file]
Date Received: [on file]
Tumor Content (estimated): 55% (assessed by H&E review of submitted FFPE block)
Test Performed: FoundationOne CDx — Comprehensive Genomic Profiling (CGP) covering 324 cancer-related genes (full coding exons), select intronic regions for rearrangement detection, and genome-wide copy number analysis. Includes tumor mutational burden (TMB) and microsatellite instability (MSI) assessment.
Assay Platform: Illumina HiSeq (paired-end sequencing); mean coverage >500x

——————————————————————————————————————————
GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY
——————————————————————————————————————————

ACTIONABLE / CLINICALLY RELEVANT ALTERATIONS

1. TP53 — p.R248W (c.742C>T) | Missense Mutation | Pathogenic
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48%
   Classification: Somatic, pathogenic gain-of-function/dominant-negative
   Notes: Hotspot mutation at codon 248, one of the most frequently altered residues in the TP53 tumor suppressor gene. The p.R248W substitution abolishes direct DNA contact, resulting in loss of sequence-specific DNA binding and transcriptional activation of p53 target genes. This mutation also confers dominant-negative suppression of wild-type p53 and gain-of-function oncogenic activity. Present in the majority of triple-negative breast cancers (>80%); not a current direct therapeutic target but informs tumor biology and platinum sensitivity. VAF consistent with clonal, truncal mutation.

2. PTEN — Homozygous Deletion | Copy Number Loss | Pathogenic
   Copy Number: 0 (homozygous, bi-allelic deletion)
   Chromosomal Location: 10q23.31
   Classification: Somatic, loss-of-function, pathogenic
   Notes: Complete loss of PTEN protein function results in constitutive activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. PTEN loss is identified in approximately 25-35% of TNBC cases and correlates with increased PI3K pathway dependency. In the absence of concurrent PIK3CA activating mutation, PTEN loss is the predominant mechanism of PI3K pathway activation in this tumor. This alteration may confer sensitivity to PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors (e.g., ipatasertib, capivasertib) and may influence response to immune checkpoint blockade. Not currently actionable per FDA-approved indication in the breast cancer setting but relevant for clinical trial consideration.

NO ALTERATIONS DETECTED
   PIK3CA: No mutation detected (wild-type)
   BRCA1 (tumor): No mutation detected (wild-type; see germline note below)
   BRCA2 (tumor): No mutation detected (wild-type; see germline note below)
   AKT1: No mutation detected (wild-type)
   ESR1: No mutation detected (wild-type)
   ERBB2 (HER2): No amplification or mutation detected (consistent with IHC/FISH negative; HER2/CEP17 ratio 1.2 per concurrent FISH)

——————————————————————————————————————————
ADDITIONAL SOMATIC ALTERATIONS OF POTENTIAL RELEVANCE
——————————————————————————————————————————

3. MYC — Amplification | High-Level Copy Number Gain | Potentially Pathogenic
   Copy Number: 18 (high-level amplification)
   Chromosomal Location: 8q24.21
   Classification: Somatic, gain-of-function
   Notes: MYC amplification is identified in approximately 25-40% of TNBC and is associated with increased transcriptional output, accelerated cell cycle progression, and resistance to apoptosis. High-level MYC amplification (≥6 copies) is independently associated with aggressive clinical behavior and reduced disease-free survival in TNBC. No approved MYC-directed therapies are currently available; however, MYC-amplified tumors may show heightened sensitivity to BET bromodomain inhibitors and CDK7 inhibitors in investigational settings. Noted as a genomic feature of high-risk disease biology.

4. RB1 — p.R579* (c.1735C>T) | Nonsense Mutation | Pathogenic (Loss-of-Function)
   VAF: 42%
   Chromosomal Location: 13q14.2
   Copy Number: Loss (concurrent with sequence mutation, consistent with biallelic inactivation)
   Notes: RB1 encodes the retinoblastoma tumor suppressor protein, a central regulator of the G1/S cell cycle checkpoint. Biallelic inactivation (nonsense mutation + allelic loss) results in constitutive CDK-independent cell cycle progression. RB1 loss is present in approximately 20-25% of TNBC and is associated with primary resistance to CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib); this is not clinically relevant given the ER-negative context but is noted. RB1 loss may sensitize tumors to platinum-based chemotherapy and topoisomerase inhibitors. This alteration, in conjunction with CDKN2A deletion (below), confirms a markedly disrupted cell cycle control apparatus.

5. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion | Copy Number Loss | Pathogenic (Loss-of-Function)
   Copy Number: 0 (homozygous, bi-allelic deletion)
   Chromosomal Location: 9p21.3
   Notes: Homozygous deletion of CDKN2A results in simultaneous loss of both p16INK4a (a CDK4/6 inhibitor and tumor suppressor) and p14ARF (an activator of p53 via MDM2 inhibition). Co-occurrence of CDKN2A deletion with TP53 mutation is a recognized feature of highly aggressive, cell cycle-dysregulated TNBC. This finding further reinforces the high proliferative index (Ki-67 85%) and grade 3 histology.

6. CDK6 — Amplification | Copy Number Gain
   Copy Number: 7
   Chromosomal Location: 7q21.2
   Notes: CDK6 amplification may provide a compensatory cell cycle activation mechanism independent of CDKN2A status, further driving cyclin D-CDK6 complex activity. In the setting of concurrent RB1 loss (above), CDK6 amplification is not expected to confer sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitors. No current approved indications in TNBC.

7. KMT2C (MLL3) — p.E1285* (c.3853G>T) | Nonsense Mutation | Likely Pathogenic
   VAF: 31%
   Notes: KMT2C encodes a histone H3 lysine 4 (H3K4) monomethyltransferase, a component of the COMPASS-like complex involved in transcriptional regulation and enhancer activation. Loss-of-function mutations in KMT2C are among the most frequent chromatin-remodeling alterations in breast cancer across subtypes (~10-15% TNBC). Functional consequences include altered enhancer landscapes and transcriptional deregulation. Not currently actionable but may influence epigenetic-based therapeutic strategies.

8. ARID1A — p.Q1378* (c.4132C>T) | Nonsense Mutation | Likely Pathogenic
   VAF: 28%
   Notes: ARID1A encodes a SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex subunit (BAF250a). Loss-of-function mutations disrupt nucleosome positioning and transcriptional programs. ARID1A mutations co-occur with PI3K pathway alterations (consistent with PTEN loss in this case) and may confer synthetic lethality with EZH2 inhibitors or PARP inhibitors in certain experimental contexts. Frequency ~10-12% in TNBC. No approved therapies.

9. SPEN — p.Q1690fs (c.5068_5069delAG) | Frameshift Deletion | Likely Pathogenic (Loss-of-Function)
   VAF: 22%
   Notes: SPEN (also known as SHARP or MINT) encodes a transcriptional repressor involved in Notch signaling and nuclear hormone receptor co-repressor activity. Loss-of-function mutations may modulate Notch signaling pathway activity (see NOTCH1 below). The lower VAF (22%) suggests this may be a subclonal alteration arising in a tumor subpopulation.

10. FAT1 — p.R2428H (c.7283G>A) | Missense Mutation | Variant of Unknown Significance
    VAF: 19%
    Notes: FAT1 encodes an atypical cadherin involved in Wnt/β-catenin signaling regulation and Hippo pathway modulation. Recurrent FAT1 mutations are seen across multiple tumor types including breast cancer (~8%). The p.R2428H substitution is not a well-characterized hotspot; functional significance is uncertain. Subclonal (VAF 19%).

11. NF1 — p.E1803* (c.5407G>T) | Nonsense Mutation | Likely Pathogenic (Loss-of-Function)
    VAF: 18%
    Notes: NF1 encodes neurofibromin, a RAS-GTPase activating protein (RAS-GAP). Loss-of-function mutations lead to constitutive RAS activation and downstream MAPK and PI3K pathway signaling. NF1 mutations are identified in ~10-15% of TNBC and, in combination with PTEN loss, may create a convergent state of both PI3K/AKT and RAS/MAPK pathway activation. Subclonal (VAF 18%); possibly a secondary event in tumor evolution. MEK inhibitor sensitivity has been reported in NF1-deficient preclinical models.

12. NOTCH1 — p.P2461fs (c.7381_7382insA) | Frameshift Insertion | Likely Pathogenic (Loss-of-Function)
    VAF: 15%
    Notes: NOTCH1 frameshift mutation predicts premature truncation and loss of the transcriptional activation domain. In the context of concurrent SPEN loss (above), this suggests dysregulation of the Notch signaling axis in a subpopulation of tumor cells. The low VAF (15%) indicates a subclonal, likely late event in tumor evolution. NOTCH pathway alterations are present in ~10% of TNBC.

——————————————————————————————————————————
COPY NUMBER ALTERATION SUMMARY
——————————————————————————————————————————

Gains / Amplifications:
  - MYC (8q24.21): High-level amplification, copy number = 18
  - CDK6 (7q21.2): Low-level amplification, copy number = 7

Losses / Deletions:
  - PTEN (10q23.31): Homozygous deletion, copy number = 0
  - CDKN2A (9p21.3): Homozygous deletion, copy number = 0
  - RB1 (13q14.2): Hemizygous to homozygous loss (in context of nonsense mutation = biallelic inactivation)

Genome-Wide Copy Number Profile:
  Ploidy estimate: ~3.2 (near-triploid with focal alterations)
  Fraction of genome altered (FGA): 0.28 (28% of genome with somatic copy number alterations)
  Overall copy number instability is high, consistent with TP53-mutant, grade 3 TNBC biology.

——————————————————————————————————————————
FUSIONS AND STRUCTURAL REARRANGEMENTS
——————————————————————————————————————————
  No gene fusions or structural rearrangements detected.
  NTRK1/2/3: No fusion detected
  ALK: No fusion detected
  ROS1: No fusion detected
  FGFR1/2/3: No fusion or rearrangement detected
  MET: No exon 14 skipping; no amplification

——————————————————————————————————————————
BIOMARKERS FOR IMMUNOTHERAPY
——————————————————————————————————————————

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):
  TMB = 8 mutations/Mb (Intermediate)
  Classification: TMB-Low/Intermediate (threshold for TMB-High designation: ≥10 mut/Mb)
  Notes: TMB of 8 mut/Mb does not meet the FDA-approved threshold (≥10 mut/Mb) for tumor-agnostic pembrolizumab eligibility per KEYNOTE-158. However, this value does not preclude immunotherapy benefit in PD-L1-positive TNBC, where efficacy in the neoadjuvant/perioperative setting (KEYNOTE-522) is not TMB-gated. The moderate TMB may reflect the relatively high somatic mutation burden typical of basal-like/TNBC tumors but is not in the hypermutated range, consistent with APOBEC-related mutagenesis rather than microsatellite instability or mismatch repair deficiency.

Microsatellite Instability (MSI):
  MSI Status: Microsatellite Stable (MSS)
  Method: Fragment length analysis of 95 microsatellite loci
  Notes: MSS status is consistent with proficient mismatch repair (pMMR) and excludes MSI-H/dMMR as a mechanism of immune activation in this tumor. MSI-H-directed pembrolizumab indications (tumor-agnostic) do not apply.

PD-L1 (by IHC, 22C3 PharmDx):
  Combined Positive Score (CPS): 18
  Interpretation: POSITIVE (threshold ≥10 for pembrolizumab eligibility in TNBC per KEYNOTE-522 and KEYNOTE-355)
  Notes: PD-L1 CPS of 18 supports pembrolizumab eligibility in the neoadjuvant and metastatic TNBC settings. This result is reported from the IHC performed on the same core biopsy specimen and is concordant with the submitted pathology report.

——————————————————————————————————————————
HOMOLOGOUS RECOMBINATION DEFICIENCY (HRD) ASSESSMENT
——————————————————————————————————————————

HRD Score (Myriad myChoice CDx-equivalent genomic instability score):
  HRD Score: 42 (composite of LOH score + telomeric allelic imbalance + large-scale state transitions)
  Threshold for HRD-High: ≥42.5 (Myriad/FDA definition for olaparib/niraparib eligibility in gBRCA-negative HRD+ tumors)
  Classification: BORDERLINE / HRD-NEGATIVE (score below threshold by 0.5 points)
  Notes: This tumor narrowly misses the genomic instability threshold for olaparib eligibility in the gBRCA-negative context per OlympiA trial / FDA label. The borderline HRD score, in the absence of somatic or germline BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutations, and with a wildtype RAD51 pathway, does not support PARP inhibitor eligibility at this time. However, the presence of PTEN loss and relatively elevated chromosomal instability suggest that a subset of homologous recombination pathway genes may have functional impairment beyond what is captured by the current biomarker assay. Retesting or alternative HRD assays may be considered if clinically relevant.

BRCA1 Promoter Methylation: Not detected (assessed by methylation-specific PCR)

——————————————————————————————————————————
GERMLINE NOTE
——————————————————————————————————————————
  This assay is a somatic tumor-only comprehensive genomic profile. Germline variants cannot be definitively distinguished from somatic variants in the absence of a paired normal sample. Variants with high VAF (approaching 50%) and/or known germline associations were reflexed for germline consideration.

  Germline panel (reported separately, constitutional DNA):
    BRCA1: No pathogenic variant detected (wild-type)
    BRCA2: No pathogenic variant detected (wild-type)
    PALB2: No pathogenic variant detected (wild-type)
    CHEK2: No pathogenic variant detected (wild-type)
    ATM: p.V2424G (c.7271T>G) — Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
      VAF in tumor: consistent with germline (heterozygous)
      Classification: VUS; insufficient evidence for pathogenicity reclassification at this time
      Notes: ATM p.V2424G is a rare missense variant affecting the FATC domain of the ATM kinase. This domain is critical for ATM kinase activity and DNA double-strand break signaling. The variant has not been reported in ClinVar with sufficient frequency to establish pathogenicity; functional data are limited. Genetic counseling is recommended. Clinical management changes based solely on this VUS are not currently supported by professional guidelines (NCCN, ACMG). Patient and family members should be informed that variant reclassification may occur as more data accumulate.

——————————————————————————————————————————
INTEGRATED GENOMIC INTERPRETATION
——————————————————————————————————————————

This tumor harbors a genomic profile highly characteristic of aggressive, treatment-refractory triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) of the basal-like molecular subtype. The co-occurrence of the following features defines a high-risk genomic landscape:

  1. TP53 p.R248W (hotspot, clonal): Confirms p53 pathway inactivation as a truncal event, consistent with essentially universal TP53 alteration in TNBC. The dominant-negative and gain-of-function properties of this specific substitution may additionally promote oncogenic transcriptional programs.

  2. PTEN homozygous deletion + PI3K pathway activation: In the absence of PIK3CA and AKT1 mutations, PTEN loss is the dominant PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activating mechanism. This may serve as a rationale for clinical trial enrollment targeting AKT (e.g., AKT inhibitors ipatasertib or capivasertib in combination with chemotherapy or immunotherapy).

  3. Cell cycle dysregulation (RB1 biallelic inactivation + CDKN2A homozygous deletion + CDK6 amplification + MYC amplification): The compound disruption of the G1/S checkpoint through independent mechanisms (loss of both major checkpoint suppressors RB1 and p16INK4a, combined with amplification of their activators CDK6 and MYC) indicates extreme proliferative capacity and near-complete abrogation of cell cycle checkpoints. Ki-67 85% is the phenotypic correlate of this genomic state.

  4. Chromatin remodeling alterations (KMT2C, ARID1A): Concurrent loss-of-function in two SWI/SNF and COMPASS-related chromatin regulators suggests epigenetic deregulation as a contributor to malignant transcriptional programs. These alterations may potentiate inflammatory/immunogenic gene expression and could partially account for the elevated TIL score (40%) and PD-L1 positivity (CPS 18) observed on pathology.

  5. Subclonal heterogeneity (SPEN, FAT1, NF1, NOTCH1 at VAF 15-22%): The presence of multiple subclonal alterations involving the Notch, RAS, and Wnt pathways indicates tumor evolutionary complexity. This heterogeneity may facilitate adaptive resistance under therapeutic selective pressure and is consistent with the high genomic instability observed on copy number analysis.

  6. Borderline HRD (score 42, just below threshold): While formal HRD-high designation is not met, the elevated chromosomal structural instability (28% genome altered, near-triploid ploidy, multiple focal deletions) is consistent with a tumor with at least partial HR pathway impairment. Platinum-based chemotherapy sensitivity may be greater than average for TNBC, even in the absence of BRCA mutation.

  7. PD-L1 CPS 18 (positive): Supports immunotherapy eligibility and is biologically consistent with the elevated TIL score and chromatin remodeling defects. The immune-inflamed tumor microenvironment may potentiate response to pembrolizumab-based regimens.

SUMMARY STATEMENT:
Comprehensive genomic profiling reveals a highly complex, clonally evolved TNBC genome with truncal TP53 mutation (p.R248W), PTEN homozygous deletion, high-level MYC amplification, biallelic RB1 inactivation, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion as the dominant oncogenic drivers. PD-L1 CPS 18 supports immunotherapy eligibility. TMB is intermediate (8 mut/Mb), MSS confirmed. HRD score is borderline (42, below 42.5 threshold). No germline BRCA1/2/PALB2 pathogenic variants identified; ATM p.V2424G remains a VUS. No actionable fusions or rearrangements identified. The overall genomic profile is consistent with high-grade, highly aggressive TNBC and provides biological rationale for pembrolizumab-containing neoadjuvant regimens (e.g., KEYNOTE-522). Clinical trial consideration for AKT/PI3K pathway inhibitors is supported given PTEN loss.

——————————————————————————————————————————
QUALITY METRICS
——————————————————————————————————————————
  Tumor purity: 55% (adequate for analysis)
  Mean sequencing depth: 538x (exceeds minimum threshold of 250x)
  Coverage uniformity: 98.4% of targeted bases at >100x
  DNA input: 112 ng (exceeds minimum 50 ng threshold)
  Sample QC: PASS
  All variant calls meet internal quality filters (MAF, strand bias, base quality, mapping quality)

——————————————————————————————————————————
TEST METHODOLOGY NOTE
——————————————————————————————————————————
FoundationOne CDx is an FDA-approved in vitro diagnostic device for comprehensive tumor profiling. This test was performed at a CAP-accredited, CLIA-certified laboratory. Results should be interpreted in the context of the patient's complete clinical and pathological information by a qualified physician.

Reporting Pathologist / Medical Director: [Molecular Pathology, Foundation Medicine]
Report Verified: [Date on file]

——————————————————————————————————————————
END OF REPORT
——————————————————————————————————————————
